summary: Drew DeVault provides his January 2020 status update covering progress across multiple projects. Sway 1.3 is nearing release with VNC support, while the Sedna project will serve as a proving ground for a new wlroots scene graph API. SourceHut has received significant performance improvements and new public monitoring infrastructure. He's also working on himitsu (a secret store), imrsh (an interactive POSIX shell), and teases an unnamed secret project.
key_points: Sway 1.3 RC3 is imminent with VNC support and input latency improvements, while Sedna will prove out the new wlroots scene graph API incorporating libliftoff
SourceHut's git.sr.ht received major performance upgrades via new hardware, and the Q4 2019 financial report and public monitoring were published
Himitsu, a security-sensitive secret store, has most crypto code written but is being developed cautiously with known bugs to resolve
imrsh aims to be a comfortable interactive shell with fish-like UX but strict POSIX syntax, built atop Simon Ser's nearly-complete mrsh
money_quotes: We've decided to use it as a proving grounds for the new wlroots scene graph API, which plans to incorporate Simon Ser's libliftoff and put to rest the eternal debate over how wlroots renderer should take shape.

This'll be lots of work but the result will be a remarkably good foundation on which we can run performant compositors on a huge variety of devices — and, if we're lucky, might help resolve the Nvidia problem.

The latest round of performance improvements for git.sr.ht finally landed with the introduction of new server hardware, and it's finally competitive with its peers in terms of push and web performance.
I'm taking it pretty slowly because it's a security-sensitive project.
A comfortable interactive shell, inspired by fish's interactive mode, but with a strictly POSIX syntax.
key_insight: DeVault's January 2020 update showcases a prolific open-source ecosystem spanning Wayland compositors, developer infrastructure, security tools, and shell implementations, all advancing incrementally through collaborative free software development.
